answer:
Async/Await at Scale — Architecture Strategy

📘 Definition

Async/Await is syntactic sugar over Promises providing synchronous-looking control flow for asynchronous operations. At 1M+ users, scalable async/await architecture focuses on structured concurrency (ensuring all spawned tasks complete or cancel together), error propagation boundaries, timeout management, and preventing common pitfalls like unintentional serialization and zombie async operations that outlive their parent scope.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

The HR system's leave approval workflow involved 5 sequential async steps: validate → check balance → create record → notify manager via Socket.io → update calendar. During a production incident, the Socket.io notification step hung for 30s (server was overloaded), causing the entire approval chain to block. Manoj implemented a structured concurrency pattern with per-step timeouts (AbortController + Promise.race), ensuring no single step could hold up the pipeline beyond 5s. Non-critical steps (notifications) were moved to a fire-and-forget pattern with error logging, while critical steps (balance check, record creation) retained strict await with circuit breakers.

📊 Structured Async/Await Pipeline

💻 Code Example — Structured Concurrency with Timeouts

// Utility: timeout-aware async operation
async function withTimeout(
  fn: () => Promise,
  timeoutMs: number,
  label: string
): Promise {
  const controller = new AbortController();
  const timeout = setTimeout(() => controller.abort(), timeoutMs);

  try {
    const result = await fn();
    return result;
  } catch (err) {
    if (controller.signal.aborted) {
      throw new TimeoutError(`${label} timed out after ${timeoutMs}ms`);
    }
    throw err;
  } finally {
    clearTimeout(timeout);
  }
}

// Leave approval with structured concurrency
async function approveLeave(leaveId: string) {
  // Critical path — strict await with timeouts
  const leave = await withTimeout(
    () => prisma.leave.findUnique({ where: { id: leaveId } }),
    2000, 'validate'
  );

  const balance = await withTimeout(
    () => checkBalance(leave.employeeId, leave.type),
    3000, 'balance-check'
  );

  const record = await withTimeout(
    () => prisma.leave.update({
      where: { id: leaveId },
      data: { status: 'approved', approvedAt: new Date() }
    }),
    5000, 'create-record'
  );

  // Non-critical — fire-and-forget with error logging
  notifyManager(leave.managerId, record).catch(err =>
    logger.warn('Notification failed (non-critical)', { err, leaveId })
  );

  updateCalendar(leave).catch(err =>
    logger.warn('Calendar sync failed (non-critical)', { err, leaveId })
  );

  return record;
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

After deploying a Socket.io upgrade, the HR system's approval endpoint started timing out intermittently. Fastify request logs showed some approvals taking 35s — far beyond the 10s client timeout. Using Node.js async_hooks and the why-is-node-running package, Manoj traced the hang to step 4 (notify manager via Socket.io). The new Socket.io version had a changed reconnection backoff that caused emit to block when the connection was in reconnecting state. He split the pipeline into critical (steps 1-3, strict await) and non-critical (steps 4-5, fire-and-forget), added per-step timeouts, and instrumented each step with performance.mark. Post-fix: zero timeouts, and the Grafana dashboard showed the non-critical notification failures clearly without affecting user experience.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Missing await on async function callsFire-and-forget when await was intended; lost errorsTypeScript @typescript-eslint/no-floating-promises rule
Async operations outliving parent scopeZombie tasks consuming resources after request endsAbortController passed to all async ops; cancel on request close
try/catch around await swallowing errorsSilent failures, data corruptionRe-throw after logging; typed error boundaries per operation
Sequential awaits in loopsO(n) latency instead of O(1) parallelUse Promise.all(items.map(...)) with concurrency limit

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Per-step timing: performance.mark per async step; alert if any step exceeds its timeout budget
  • Structured concurrency test: Integration test verifying that parent abort cancels all child operations
  • Zombie detection: why-is-node-running in CI to detect async handles that shouldn't persist
  • ESLint enforcement: no-floating-promises + no-await-in-loop in CI
  • Load test: k6 with 100 concurrent approvals; P99 < 1s, zero timeouts

Async/Await — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

📘 Definition

Async/Await performance bottlenecks include unintentional serialization of parallelizable operations, excessive microtask scheduling overhead, stack trace loss in async chains, and memory retention from suspended async function frames. Instrumentation involves async profiling, custom performance timing, and structured logging to detect sequential-await anti-patterns, measure actual vs. optimal concurrency, and trace slow async operations through the call chain.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

ContactsHub's bulk CSV upload processed 500 contacts per batch. The original implementation used a for...of loop with await inside — each contact was validated, image-processed (Canvas API crop), and saved to MongoDB sequentially, taking 45s for 500 contacts. Manoj instrumented each step with performance.now() and discovered that image processing (Cloudinary upload) was the bottleneck at 80ms/contact. He refactored to Promise.all with p-limit(10) for controlled concurrency, reducing total processing time to 4.5s — a 10x improvement. The instrumentation dashboard tracked per-batch timing, failure rates, and Cloudinary API latency percentiles.

📊 Async/Await Bottleneck Analysis

💻 Code Example — Instrumented Parallel Async Processing

import pLimit from 'p-limit';

// ❌ Sequential: 45s for 500 contacts
async function processContactsSequential(contacts: Contact[]) {
  const results = [];
  for (const contact of contacts) {
    const validated = await validateContact(contact);  // 5ms
    const image = await cropAndUpload(contact.photo);  // 80ms
    const saved = await db.contacts.create({           // 5ms
      data: { ...validated, photoUrl: image.url }
    });
    results.push(saved);
  }
  return results; // 500 × 90ms = 45,000ms
}

// ✅ Controlled parallel: 4.5s for 500 contacts
async function processContactsParallel(contacts: Contact[]) {
  const limit = pLimit(10);
  const timings = { validate: [], crop: [], save: [] };

  const results = await Promise.allSettled(
    contacts.map(contact => limit(async () => {
      const t0 = performance.now();
      const validated = await validateContact(contact);
      timings.validate.push(performance.now() - t0);

      const t1 = performance.now();
      const image = await cropAndUpload(contact.photo);
      timings.crop.push(performance.now() - t1);

      const t2 = performance.now();
      const saved = await db.contacts.create({
        data: { ...validated, photoUrl: image.url }
      });
      timings.save.push(performance.now() - t2);

      return saved;
    }))
  );

  // Log timing percentiles
  const p95 = (arr) => arr.sort((a,b) => a-b)[Math.floor(arr.length * 0.95)];
  console.log('P95 timings:', {
    validate: p95(timings.validate).toFixed(1) + 'ms',
    crop: p95(timings.crop).toFixed(1) + 'ms',
    save: p95(timings.save).toFixed(1) + 'ms'
  });

  const succeeded = results.filter(r => r.status === 'fulfilled').length;
  const failed = results.filter(r => r.status === 'rejected').length;
  console.log(`Processed: ${succeeded} success, ${failed} failed`);

  return results;
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Users reported the bulk import "freezing" when uploading 500 contacts. Manoj added per-step instrumentation and found Cloudinary uploads dominated at 80ms P50 (150ms P95). But the real bottleneck was the sequential loop — 500 × 90ms = 45s, with only 11% CPU utilization (waiting on network I/O). After switching to p-limit(10), CPU utilization jumped to 65% and total time dropped to 4.5s. He experimented with concurrency levels: 5 (8s), 10 (4.5s), 20 (4.2s but Cloudinary rate-limited), settling on 10 as the sweet spot. A Playwright test validated the full flow: upload 500-contact CSV → assert completion < 10s → verify all contacts in DB.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
await inside for loopsSerial execution of parallel-safe operationsUse Promise.all + p-limit; ESLint no-await-in-loop
Lost stack traces in async chainsErrors show only partial call stackUse Error.captureStackTrace; enable --async-stack-traces
Uncapped concurrency with Promise.allAPI rate limits, connection exhaustionAlways use concurrency limiter; tune via load testing
Async function frame retentionSuspended frames hold references, increasing heapAvoid capturing large objects before long awaits; null early

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Per-step P95 instrumentation: Logged to monitoring; alert if any step degrades 50%+ from baseline
  • Concurrency tuning test: Automated script testing concurrency levels 5/10/20 against Cloudinary; document optimal level
  • Playwright E2E: Upload 500-contact CSV → complete < 10s → all contacts in DB
  • Rate limit detection: Monitor Cloudinary 429 responses; auto-reduce concurrency if rate exceeded
  • CPU utilization check: If CPU < 20% during batch operations, likely a serialization anti-pattern

Type Narrowing at Scale — Architecture Strategy

📘 Definition

Type Narrowing is TypeScript's mechanism for refining types within conditional blocks using type guards, discriminated unions, and control flow analysis. At scale, type narrowing architecture ensures compile-time safety across shared models, API boundaries, and polymorphic data structures. A scalable strategy uses discriminated unions for domain events, branded types for ID safety, and assertion functions for runtime validation that narrows types at system boundaries — preventing the "any escape hatch" that erodes type safety in large codebases.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

The HR system handled 6 leave types (annual, sick, maternity, paternity, compensatory, unpaid) each with different validation rules and approval chains. Initially modeled as type: string with runtime checks scattered across 15 files. Manoj refactored to a discriminated union with per-type narrowing, catching 8 bugs at compile time (e.g., maternity leave being processed through the standard 3-level approval instead of the mandatory HR-direct approval). The type-narrowed approach eliminated all runtime type-check errors and reduced leave-processing bugs from 3/month to 0 over 6 months.

📊 Discriminated Union Architecture

💻 Code Example — Discriminated Union with Exhaustive Narrowing

// Discriminated union for leave types
interface AnnualLeave {
  type: 'annual';
  days: number;
  approvalChain: [string, string, string]; // 3-level
}

interface MaternityLeave {
  type: 'maternity';
  expectedDueDate: Date;
  hrApprover: string; // HR-direct
  medicalCertificate: string;
}

interface SickLeave {
  type: 'sick';
  days: number;
  medicalCertificate?: string; // required if > 2 days
}

type LeaveRequest = AnnualLeave | MaternityLeave | SickLeave;

// Exhaustive narrowing — compiler enforces all cases handled
function processLeave(leave: LeaveRequest): ApprovalResult {
  switch (leave.type) {
    case 'annual':
      // TypeScript knows: leave is AnnualLeave
      return startApprovalChain(leave.approvalChain, leave.days);

    case 'maternity':
      // TypeScript knows: leave is MaternityLeave
      return directHRApproval(leave.hrApprover, leave.medicalCertificate);

    case 'sick':
      // TypeScript knows: leave is SickLeave
      if (leave.days <= 2) return autoApprove();
      if (!leave.medicalCertificate) {
        throw new ValidationError('Medical certificate required for > 2 sick days');
      }
      return managerApproval(leave.medicalCertificate);

    default:
      // Exhaustive check — compiler error if new type added without handling
      const _exhaustive: never = leave;
      throw new Error(`Unhandled leave type: ${_exhaustive}`);
  }
}

// Branded types for ID safety
type EmployeeId = string & { readonly __brand: 'EmployeeId' };
type LeaveId = string & { readonly __brand: 'LeaveId' };

function createEmployeeId(id: string): EmployeeId {
  if (!id.match(/^EMP-\d{6}$/)) throw new Error('Invalid employee ID');
  return id as EmployeeId;
}

// Cannot accidentally pass LeaveId where EmployeeId expected
function getEmployee(id: EmployeeId) { /* ... */ }

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

A bug report showed maternity leave requests going through the standard 3-level approval chain instead of HR-direct approval. The root cause: the original code used leave.type === 'maternity' checks in some files but not others. When Manoj refactored to discriminated unions, the TypeScript compiler immediately flagged 8 locations where the maternity case was missing from switch statements (the never exhaustive check caught them). After the refactoring, adding a new leave type (compensatory) caused compiler errors in exactly the 15 files that needed updating — a task that previously would have required grep-and-pray.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Using as type assertions instead of narrowingBypasses compiler checks, runtime errorsESLint @typescript-eslint/no-unnecessary-type-assertion
Missing exhaustive check in switchNew union members silently unhandledAlways add default: never exhaustive check
Type predicates with incorrect logicNarrows to wrong type, subtle runtime bugsUnit test each type guard; use assertion functions with runtime checks
Widening types at API boundariesExternal data bypasses narrowing safetyZod/io-ts validation at boundary; narrow from unknown

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Strict TypeScript config: strict: true, noUncheckedIndexedAccess: true in tsconfig
  • Exhaustive check coverage: Every switch on union types must have default: never
  • Type guard unit tests: Each custom type predicate tested with positive/negative cases
  • Boundary validation: Zod schemas at API endpoints validating incoming data matches union types
  • CI type-check: tsc --noEmit in CI pipeline; zero tolerance for type errors

Type Narrowing — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

📘 Definition

Type Narrowing performance bottlenecks are primarily compile-time concerns: deeply nested conditional types, complex generic constraints, and large discriminated unions can slow TypeScript compilation. At runtime, excessive type guard checks add overhead, and poorly structured narrowing can prevent bundler optimizations. Instrumentation involves measuring tsc build times, tracing slow type computations, and benchmarking runtime guard performance to keep both developer experience and application performance optimal.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's CRM Dashboard

The CRM Dashboard's type system grew to include a 24-variant discriminated union for sales pipeline events (lead created, contacted, qualified, proposal sent, etc.). TypeScript compilation slowed from 8s to 45s as conditional types recursively computed derived types for each variant. Manoj profiled tsc with --generateTrace and identified a recursive conditional type that expanded to 2^24 type instantiations. He refactored to a lookup-map pattern (Record<EventType, Handler>) that kept compile time at 12s while maintaining full type safety. Runtime type guards were also benchmarked: replacing a chain of 24 instanceof checks with a single discriminant property lookup reduced guard overhead from 0.8μs to 0.05μs per check.

📊 Type Narrowing Performance Impact

💻 Code Example — Optimized Type Narrowing Patterns

// ❌ Slow: recursive conditional type (exponential instantiation)
type DeepNarrow = T extends { type: infer U }
  ? U extends string
    ? { [K in U]: Extract }
    : never
  : never;

// ✅ Fast: lookup-map pattern (linear instantiation)
type EventType = 'lead_created' | 'contacted' | 'qualified' | 'proposal_sent';

interface EventMap {
  lead_created: { leadId: string; source: string };
  contacted: { leadId: string; channel: 'email' | 'phone' };
  qualified: { leadId: string; score: number };
  proposal_sent: { leadId: string; amount: number };
}

// Type-safe handler map — O(n) not O(2^n) instantiation
type EventHandlers = {
  [K in EventType]: (event: EventMap[K]) => void;
};

const handlers: EventHandlers = {
  lead_created: (e) => console.log(e.source),     // e is typed
  contacted: (e) => console.log(e.channel),        // e is typed
  qualified: (e) => console.log(e.score),           // e is typed
  proposal_sent: (e) => console.log(e.amount),     // e is typed
};

// Runtime: discriminant property lookup (0.05μs)
function dispatch(type: K, data: EventMap[K]) {
  handlers[type](data); // single property access, no chain of checks
}

// Profile tsc compilation:
// npx tsc --generateTrace ./trace-output
// Open trace-output/trace.json in chrome://tracing

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

Developer feedback flagged that tsc --watch was taking 45s per recompilation, crippling the dev inner loop. Manoj ran tsc --generateTrace and loaded the trace in Chrome's tracing UI. The flame graph showed a single type — DeepNarrow<PipelineEvent> — consuming 38s of the 45s compilation. It was a recursive conditional type that computed derived types for all 24 event variants, resulting in 2^24 type instantiations. He replaced it with a Record<EventType, Payload> lookup pattern that achieved the same type safety with O(24) instantiations. Compile time dropped to 12s. He added a CI check: tsc --extendedDiagnostics | grep 'Check time' with a budget of 20s.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Recursive conditional typesExponential type instantiation, slow compilationUse lookup-map patterns; limit recursion depth with counters
Large union type narrowingTypeScript re-checks all variants at each narrowing pointSplit into sub-unions; use discriminant-based narrowing
Overuse of infer in nested positionsType checker explores many branchesSimplify inference to top-level; use explicit type parameters
Runtime type guard chains (instanceof)O(n) checks per narrowing, measurable in hot pathsUse discriminant property (O(1)); avoid prototype-based checks

✅ Validation Strategy

  • tsc --extendedDiagnostics: CI gate on check time < 20s
  • tsc --generateTrace: Monthly review of type computation flame graphs
  • Type instantiation count: @typescript-eslint/no-misused-promises and custom rules for deep conditionals
  • Runtime guard benchmark: Vitest bench for type guards in hot paths; < 0.1μs per check
  • Developer experience survey: Track IDE responsiveness (go-to-definition latency < 500ms)

Generics at Scale — Architecture Strategy

📘 Definition

Generics enable type-parameterized abstractions in TypeScript — reusable components, functions, and data structures that maintain type safety across diverse use cases. At 1M+ users, scalable generic architecture focuses on creating shared utility types for API responses, form handling, and state management that eliminate boilerplate while preserving end-to-end type inference. Key challenges include constraint design (preventing over-generic APIs), inference reliability across deep generic chains, and compile-time performance with complex generic instantiations.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's HR Leave System

The HR system had 12 Fastify REST endpoints following the same pattern: validate input → query Prisma → transform response. Each endpoint duplicated type definitions for request/response shapes. Manoj created a generic API handler factory createEndpoint<TInput, TOutput> that accepted a Zod schema for input validation and a Prisma query type for output, automatically inferring the full request-to-response type chain. This reduced endpoint boilerplate by 60% (from ~80 lines to ~30 lines per endpoint) while catching 5 response-shape mismatches at compile time that had previously caused frontend rendering bugs.

📊 Generic API Handler Architecture

💻 Code Example — Generic Endpoint Factory

import { z, ZodSchema } from 'zod';
import { FastifyInstance, FastifyRequest, FastifyReply } from 'fastify';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';

// Generic endpoint factory with full type inference
function createEndpoint<
  TInput extends ZodSchema,
  TOutput
>(
  config: {
    method: 'GET' | 'POST' | 'PUT' | 'DELETE';
    path: string;
    schema: TInput;
    handler: (
      input: z.infer,
      prisma: PrismaClient
    ) => Promise;
  }
) {
  return (app: FastifyInstance, prisma: PrismaClient) => {
    app.route({
      method: config.method,
      url: config.path,
      handler: async (
        req: FastifyRequest<{ Body: z.infer }>,
        reply: FastifyReply
      ) => {
        // Validate input — narrows to z.infer
        const parsed = config.schema.parse(req.body);
        const result = await config.handler(parsed, prisma);
        return reply.send(result);
      }
    });
  };
}

// Usage: fully typed, 30 lines instead of 80
const createLeave = createEndpoint({
  method: 'POST',
  path: '/api/leaves',
  schema: z.object({
    employeeId: z.string().regex(/^EMP-\d{6}$/),
    type: z.enum(['annual', 'sick', 'maternity']),
    startDate: z.coerce.date(),
    endDate: z.coerce.date(),
  }),
  // TypeScript infers: input is { employeeId, type, startDate, endDate }
  handler: async (input, prisma) => {
    return prisma.leaveRequest.create({ data: input });
    // Return type automatically inferred from Prisma model
  }
});

// Generic API response wrapper
type ApiResponse = 
  | { success: true; data: T; timestamp: string }
  | { success: false; error: string; code: number };

function ok(data: T): ApiResponse {
  return { success: true, data, timestamp: new Date().toISOString() };
}

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

The frontend team reported that the /api/leaves endpoint returned createdAt as a string, but the TypeScript type said Date. The bug had persisted for 3 weeks because the endpoint handler manually constructed the response object without type checking against the Prisma model. After Manoj introduced the generic endpoint factory, the Prisma return type was automatically inferred as the response type. When he applied it to the leaves endpoint, TypeScript immediately flagged that the manual response construction was missing updatedAt and had createdAt typed wrong. All 12 endpoints were migrated in a single sprint, catching 5 similar mismatches.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Over-generic APIs (too many type params)Unreadable signatures, poor inferenceMax 3 type parameters; use defaults and constraints
Generic constraints too loose (extends any)No meaningful type safety gainedUse specific constraints: extends ZodSchema, extends Record
Deep generic nesting kills inferenceTypeScript falls back to unknownBreak into smaller generic functions; use explicit type arguments at boundaries
Generic instantiation performanceEach unique type parameter set creates a new instantiationMonitor tsc --extendedDiagnostics; simplify where instantiation count is high

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Type inference test: expectTypeOf (vitest) to assert generic functions infer correctly
  • Compile-time budget: tsc --extendedDiagnostics check time < 20s in CI
  • Endpoint type coverage: All 12 endpoints migrated to generic factory; no manual response construction
  • Generic constraint review: Code review checklist item: "Are type parameters constrained appropriately?"
  • Integration test: Supertest verifying response shapes match TypeScript types via Zod validation

Generics — Performance Bottlenecks & Instrumentation

📘 Definition

Generic performance bottlenecks occur at compile time (excessive type instantiations, deep inference chains, complex mapped types) and at runtime (when generic abstractions add unnecessary indirection layers). Instrumentation involves tsc --generateTrace for compile-time profiling, --extendedDiagnostics for instantiation counting, and runtime benchmarks to ensure generic wrapper overhead stays negligible compared to direct function calls.

🏢 Concrete Use-Case — Manoj's ContactsHub

ContactsHub used a generic useAsyncData<T> hook wrapping React Query for all data fetching — contacts, groups, tags, import history. With 15 unique instantiations, the hook's conditional return types (loading/error/success states) created a complex type computation. TypeScript's --extendedDiagnostics showed 4,200 type instantiations for this single hook. Manoj simplified the conditional type to a discriminated union with 3 explicit variants, dropping instantiations to 180 and cutting incremental compilation from 12s to 4s. Runtime benchmarks showed the generic wrapper added < 0.01ms overhead per call — negligible.

📊 Generic Instantiation Performance

💻 Code Example — Optimizing Generic Type Performance

// ❌ Slow: complex conditional return type
type AsyncState = 
  T extends undefined ? { loading: true } :
  T extends Error ? { error: T; loading: false } :
  { data: T; loading: false; error: null };

// Each instantiation forces TypeScript to evaluate all 3 branches
function useAsyncData(query: () => Promise): AsyncState {
  // ... implementation
}

// ✅ Fast: discriminated union (no conditional types)
type AsyncResult = 
  | { status: 'loading' }
  | { status: 'error'; error: Error }
  | { status: 'success'; data: T };

function useAsyncData(query: () => Promise): AsyncResult {
  const [state, setState] = useState>({ status: 'loading' });

  useEffect(() => {
    query()
      .then(data => setState({ status: 'success', data }))
      .catch(error => setState({ status: 'error', error }));
  }, []);

  return state;
}

// Usage: type narrows naturally via discriminant
const result = useAsyncData(() => fetchContacts());
if (result.status === 'success') {
  // TypeScript knows: result.data is Contact[]
  console.log(result.data.length);
}

// Monitor instantiation count in CI
// tsc --extendedDiagnostics 2>&1 | grep 'Instantiations'
// Assert: instantiations < 10000

🐞 Debugging Narrative — Manoj's Experience

IDE autocomplete started lagging 2-3 seconds when working in ContactsHub's components. Manoj ran tsc --extendedDiagnostics and found 4,200 type instantiations — the useAsyncData hook's conditional return type was the culprit. Each of the 15 data-fetching components instantiated the hook with a different type parameter, and each instantiation required TypeScript to evaluate all conditional branches. The discriminated union refactor was a drop-in replacement that maintained the same API while eliminating the conditional type evaluation. He added a CI script: tsc --extendedDiagnostics 2>&1 | grep Instantiations with a threshold of 10,000 to prevent future regressions.

⚠️ Pitfalls & Mitigations

PitfallImpactMitigation
Conditional types in generic returnsExponential instantiation for each type parameter combinationReplace with discriminated unions; use --generateTrace to verify
Mapped types over large unionsO(n) instantiations per mapped type applicationPre-compute mapped types; cache in type aliases
Generic constraints with keyof on large objectsType checker enumerates all keys at each usageExtract key subsets; use Pick to limit scope
Generic abstraction overhead at runtimeExtra function call layers, harder to optimize by V8Benchmark critical paths; inline if overhead is measurable

✅ Validation Strategy

  • Instantiation budget: tsc --extendedDiagnostics in CI; fail if instantiations > 10,000
  • Trace review: Monthly tsc --generateTrace review for new slow types
  • Runtime benchmark: Vitest bench for generic wrapper vs. direct call; < 0.01ms overhead
  • IDE responsiveness: Developer survey on autocomplete latency; target < 500ms
  • Type test: expectTypeOf assertions verifying generic inference correctness
